topic: A person is asked to give a speech at their parent's retirement party
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation had arrived three weeks ago, a glossy card promising heartfelt tributes. Now, standing backstage with the microphone feeling impossibly heavy in my hand, I just wanted to disappear. My mother, bless her sentimental heart, had asked me to give a speech at her retirement party. Retirement. As if I hadn't noticed the endless cycle of obligation that followed every milestone. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, the cheap fabric of my suit suddenly feeling like sandpaper against my skin. When the host finally called my name, I forced a smile that felt brittle around the edges. I scanned the sea of expectant faces, a collective expectant hum vibrating in the air, and began to speak, every carefully chosen word tasting like ash in my mouth.

[story 2]

"So, to my parents," I muttered under my breath as I adjusted the microphone stand, the metal cold against my palms. This whole setup felt utterly draining. They’d expected some eloquent reflection, some soaring anecdote about their life achievements. What I had prepared was a tightly scripted sequence of polite acknowledgments. I glanced at my watch; I was already running five minutes behind schedule. The room was thick with forced cheer, and I could feel the tension coiling in my shoulders. When I finally opened my mouth, my voice came out higher pitched than intended, and I could practically hear the polite, vacant nodding from the audience. I just wanted to get through this tedious formality so I could escape.

[story 3]

I leaned against the wall near the refreshment table, crossing my arms tightly over my chest. The background music—some overly cheerful instrumental—seemed to be actively grating on my nerves. My parents were beaming across the room, radiating an energy I simply didn't possess right now. This was supposed to be a moment of genuine warmth, but all I could focus on was the sheer endurance required to stand here and perform this required act of public affection. I took a slow, deliberate sip of water, letting the silence stretch just long enough to amplify the feeling of profound impatience churning beneath my sternum. When the host finally prompted me, I met their gaze with an expression carefully calibrated to convey dutiful participation, a mask firmly in place.
